Idiom: Use if let for Single Pattern Match
Keywords: if let, match, Option, Result, single pattern
Description: Use if let when you only care about one variant of an enum and want to ignore others
Do: Use if let Some(x) = ... for Option; use if let Ok(x) = ... for Result; use while let for loops
Dont: Use a full match block when only one arm has logic and the other is _ => ()

Keywords: let else, guard, early return, refutable pattern
Description: Use let-else to bind a pattern or diverge (return/continue/break) in one statement
Do: Use let Some(x) = expr else { return }; for guard clauses; reduces nesting and improves readability
Dont: Use nested if-let or match for guard clauses that return/continue on the failure case

Description: Prefer iterator methods over manual index-based for loops for clarity and safety
Do: Use .iter(), .iter_mut(), .into_iter(); chain with .map(), .filter(), .collect(); use .enumerate() when index is needed
Dont: Use for i in 0..vec.len() { vec[i] } style loops that risk off-by-one and bounds check overhead

Description: Always accept &str instead of &String in function parameters for maximum flexibility
Do: Use &str for function parameters; &String auto-derefs to &str; accept impl AsRef<str> for even more flexibility
Dont: Accept &String which forces callers to have an owned String

Description: Accept &[T] instead of &Vec<T> to allow any contiguous memory (Vec, array, slice)
Do: Use &[T] for function parameters; &Vec<T> auto-derefs to &[T]; use &mut [T] for mutable slices
Dont: Accept &Vec<T> which unnecessarily constrains callers to Vec

Description: Use ..other_struct to copy remaining fields from another struct of the same type
Do: Use ..Default::default() for partial initialization; use ..existing for creating modified copies
Dont: Manually specify every field when most are the same as a default or existing instance

Description: Destructure structs and tuples directly in function parameters or let bindings for clearer code
Do: Destructure when you need only a few fields; use patterns in match, if let, and function args
Dont: Access fields through the variable when destructuring would be clearer

Description: Use to_owned() for &str -> String and clone() for String -> String; use to_string() only for Display conversion
Do: Use to_owned() on &str; clone() on String; to_string() only when Display formatting is needed
Dont: Use to_string() everywhere including on &str (goes through Display formatting which is slower)
Code Good: let s: String = "hello".to_owned();   // &str -> String (direct)\nlet s2: String = s.clone();           // String -> String\nlet n: String = 42.to_string();       // i32 -> String via Display
Code Bad: let s: String = "hello".to_string(); // works but goes through Display::fmt\n// to_owned() is more direct for &str -> String conversion

Description: Use matches!() to check if a value matches a pattern and return a boolean
Do: Use matches!(value, pattern) for boolean pattern checks in filter/any/all; supports guards with if
Dont: Use verbose match blocks that return true/false; use == for enum variant checking without data

Description: Use fn new() as the conventional constructor; use fn with_*() for alternative constructors
Do: Name the primary constructor new(); use descriptive names for alternate constructors; return Self
Dont: Use random constructor names; use build() without the builder pattern; make new() return Result

Description: Match all enum variants explicitly to get compiler warnings when new variants are added
Do: Match all variants explicitly; use #[non_exhaustive] for public enums that may grow; handle _ wildcard last
Dont: Use _ as the first resort which silently handles new variants

Description: Use .copied() for Copy types and .cloned() for Clone types to convert &T to T in iterator chains
Do: Use .copied() for &i32 -> i32 etc; use .cloned() for &String -> String; prefer copied when available
Dont: Use .map(|x| *x) or .map(|x| x.clone()) when .copied() or .cloned() is more idiomatic

Idiom: Use impl Into<T> for Flexible APIs
Description: Accept impl Into<T> in function parameters to allow callers to pass any convertible type
Do: Use impl Into<String> for owned string parameters; use impl Into<PathBuf> for path parameters
Dont: Force callers to convert types manually before calling the function

Description: Use the turbofish ::<T> syntax to specify generic types when the compiler cannot infer them
Do: Use turbofish on the method: "42".parse::<i32>(); use type annotation on the variable when turbofish is awkward
Dont: Let type inference fail and wonder why; place type annotations in random positions

Idiom: Use todo!() and unimplemented!() as Placeholders
Description: Use todo!() to mark code that needs implementation and unimplemented!() for deliberately unsupported paths
Do: Use todo!() for work in progress; use unimplemented!() for paths that should never be reached; both return ! (never type)
Dont: Return dummy values that hide incomplete code; use panic!("not implemented") without semantic meaning

Keywords: shadow, let, rebind, transform, type change
Description: Shadow a variable to convert its type while keeping the same name for readability
Do: Shadow when the new binding replaces the old one completely; use shadowing for parse -> validate -> use flows
Dont: Shadow variables with unrelated meanings; shadow mut bindings when the original is still needed

Description: Use dbg!() for quick debug printing that shows the expression, value, file, and line number
Do: Use dbg!() for temporary debugging; it prints to stderr and returns the value so it can be used inline
Dont: Leave dbg!() in production code; use println! for debugging

Idiom: Use Type Aliases for Complex Types
Description: Create type aliases to simplify complex generic types and improve readability
Do: Use type aliases for frequently used complex types; name them descriptively
Dont: Create type aliases for simple types; alias standard types with confusing names

Description: Use .collect() with turbofish to transform iterators into various collection types including Result<Vec<T>>
Do: Use .collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>() to short-circuit on first error; use .collect::<String>() for char iterators
Dont: Use manual loops to build collections from iterators; forget that collect can transform Result iterators

Description: Annotate functions and types whose return values should not be silently discarded
Do: Add #[must_use] to Result-returning functions, builders, and any function where ignoring the return is likely a bug
Dont: Apply #[must_use] to void-like functions; ignore must_use warnings
